document.title: Linking External Systems Before M1
document.purpose: Describe a simple pre-M1 scheme for linking Zoo projects to external folders and repositories without placing resource paths inside project JSON files.
author: Lion Kimbro
ChatGPT

overview.basic: Zoo should continue to treat a project as an abstract project identity, while external resources such as legacy folders and repositories are represented through separate data files.
overview.goal: Allow Zoo to notice linked external resources, report when they are available on the local machine, and offer actions such as opening a local folder.
overview.non-goal: This design is not the full M1 system. It is a small, data-centered bridge that can be implemented sooner and later translated into richer linking models.

design-principles: Do not store filesystem paths inside project JSON files.
Keep project identity separate from machine-local resource resolution.
Represent cross-system associations as data, not hard-coded procedural rules.
Use portable symbolic references where possible, and local tables where necessary.
Prefer a format that can later be translated into M1 links and tables.

local-folders-table.purpose: Map folder GUIDs to concrete local filesystem paths on the current machine.
local-folders-table.presence: Optional. It may exist on one machine and be absent on another.

sidecar-project-links.purpose: Record abstract links from Zoo projects to external resource identities such as folders or repositories.

folder.meaning: A resource identity representing a filesystem folder.
folder.resolution: Zoo may resolve the folder to a concrete local path by looking up the resource GUID in local-folders-table.json.

repo.meaning: A resource identity representing a repository.
repo.resolution-note: Repository links should not assume a fixed absolute path across machines.
repo.preferred-resolution-direction: Resolve repositories later through a machine-root-based scheme such as <machine-root:LOCAL-GITHUB-REPO-CHECKOUTS>/<repo-name> or equivalent symbolic data.

startup-behavior: On startup, Zoo should load sidecar-project-links.json if present.
On startup, Zoo should also load local-folders-table.json if present.
Zoo should keep these structures in memory as supporting link-resolution data.
If one file is missing, Zoo should continue operating with partial knowledge.
runtime-behavior: When viewing a project, Zoo should check whether sidecar-project-links.json contains any links whose 'from' field matches the project's GUID.
For each link of kind 'folder', Zoo should determine whether the linked folder GUID is present in local-folders-table.json.
If the folder GUID resolves locally, Zoo should present that linked folder as locally available and may show a button that opens the folder.
If the folder GUID does not resolve locally, Zoo should still show that the project has a folder link, but indicate that the folder is not available on this machine.
For links of kind 'repo', Zoo may initially display the existence of the link even before full machine-local repository resolution is implemented.

why-two-files.separation: sidecar-project-links.json expresses abstract cross-system associations, while local-folders-table.json expresses machine-local path resolution.
why-two-files.benefit: This preserves portability of the shared project-link data while still allowing concrete actions on machines that possess the relevant resources.
why-two-files.m1-direction: The scheme loosely resembles an M1 combination of links plus tables, making it a reasonable bridge toward a later identity-centered system.

future-translation: sidecar-project-links.json could later translate naturally into M1-style links.
local-folders-table.json could later translate naturally into an M1-style table mapping resource identity to local location.
Repository resolution may later use a corresponding local repositories table or another symbolic machine-root-based resolver.
notes: This design intentionally prefers simple JSON files over a more abstract provider architecture.
The first practical target is folder-link visibility and local folder opening.
The scheme is intentionally small so it can be implemented before larger M1 work begins.
